description: Guidance for using military records to locate ancestors, establish family relationships, and extract biographical details — covering draft registrations, service records, pension files, bounty land warrants, and headstone applications

patterns.scenario: You need to locate a male ancestor in the 1917-1918 time frame or establish his physical description and residence
patterns.era_scope: 1917-1918, applicable to virtually all men aged 18-45
patterns.interpretation: The World War I draft registration was the most comprehensive enumeration of American men since the census. Three registrations (June 1917, June 1918, September 1918) ultimately covered all men aged 18-45, approximately 24 million registrations. Even men who never served filled out a draft card. The cards record full name, date of birth, address, occupation, employer and employer's address, nearest relative, physical description (height, build, eye color, hair color), and any physical disabilities. This is often the only record with a physical description.
action_steps: Search the WWI Draft Registration Cards on Ancestry (searchable index and images) or FamilySearch (browseable images organized by state, county, and draft board) — these are NARA Record Group 163, Records of the Selective Service System

If the surname search fails, browse by draft board — determine the ancestor's draft board from their address using the city directory or census. Urban areas had many draft boards; rural counties typically had one. The cards are filed alphabetically within each board

Use the draft card to confirm or discover: exact birth date (self-reported), current address in 1917-1918, occupation and employer (with employer's address), and nearest relative with their address — the nearest relative field often reveals a wife, parent, or sibling not documented elsewhere
Cross-reference the draft card address with city directories and the 1920 census to establish residential continuity or track a move between 1917 and 1920

For the September 1918 'old man's registration' (men 18-20 and 37-45), note that these cards have a slightly different format and are sometimes cataloged separately — they cover men not included in the earlier registrations
common_mistakes: Assuming a draft registration means the person served in the military — most registrants were never called to service. The draft card is not a service record
Not searching all three registration dates — the same man might appear on multiple cards if his circumstances changed between registrations
Overlooking the 'nearest relative' field, which is one of the most genealogically valuable items on the card
source_types_to_check: city directories for draft board identification
military service records (if the person was actually called up)

patterns.scenario: You need to locate a male ancestor in the 1940-1945 time frame
patterns.era_scope: 1940-1947, all men aged 18-64 at various points
patterns.interpretation: The WWII draft registration covered even more men than WWI — six registrations between 1940 and 1947 ultimately included all men aged 18-64. The 'Old Man's Draft' of April 1942 (4th Registration) registered all men aged 45-64 who were not already registered. These cards are particularly valuable for genealogy because they provide a near-census of the male population for the gap between the 1940 census and the post-war era.
action_steps: Search the WWII Draft Registration Cards (4th Registration, 'Old Man's Draft') on Ancestry — these are the most widely available and cover men born between April 28, 1877 and February 16, 1897. They list name, address, telephone number, age, date of birth, place of birth, employer, and next of kin

For other WWII registrations (1st through 3rd and 5th through 6th), check NARA's holdings — many are still being digitized. Contact the National Personnel Records Center (NPRC) in St. Louis for service records of WWII veterans
Use the draft card's 'person who will always know your address' field — this is often a parent, spouse, or sibling and provides a relationship and address not found elsewhere
Cross-reference with the 1940 census to see the ancestor's household composition just before the war, then use the draft card to track their wartime location

For men who actually served, request the Official Military Personnel File (OMPF) from NPRC in St. Louis (nprc.nara.gov) using Standard Form 180. Note: the 1973 NPRC fire destroyed approximately 80% of Army records for personnel discharged between 1912-1964 and 75% of Air Force records for personnel discharged between 1947-1964
common_mistakes: Not knowing about the 1973 NPRC fire and its impact on WWII Army records — if a record request comes back 'fire-related,' alternative documentation strategies exist using unit records, state adjutant general files, and VA records
Assuming draft registration means service — the Old Man's Draft registrants were never called up
Not checking both Ancestry and FamilySearch, as their WWII draft card coverage differs
source_types_to_check: WWII Draft Registration Cards (4th Registration on Ancestry)

patterns.scenario: An ancestor served in the Civil War (Union or Confederate) and you want detailed family information
patterns.era_scope: 1861-1934, covering the war years through the last pension payments
patterns.interpretation: Civil War pension files are among the most genealogically rich records in American history. Union pension files (at NARA) average 30-75 pages and may include: the soldier's service history, medical examinations, marriage certificates, affidavits from family members and neighbors, evidence of children's births, widow's remarriage documentation, and detailed accounts of daily life in depositions. Confederate pensions were administered by individual states, not the federal government, and are held at state archives.
action_steps: Search the Civil War Pension Index on Ancestry (based on NARA's T288 microfilm) or Fold3 to find the application number — note both the application number and the certificate number (issued if the pension was approved)

Order the complete pension file from NARA using NATF Form 85 (for mail orders) or through Fold3's digitization-on-demand service — the full file is far more detailed than the index card. Cost is approximately $100 for mail orders; Fold3 may have some already digitized

Read depositions carefully — pension applicants and witnesses provided sworn testimony about the soldier's service, injuries, marriages, children, and post-war life. Neighbors testified to the widow's continued widowhood. These depositions are often the most vivid firsthand accounts of an ancestor's life

For Confederate pensions, contact the state archives for the state where the veteran applied — these are typically the state where the veteran lived after the war, not necessarily where they served. Major holdings: Tennessee State Library and Archives, Virginia State Library, Georgia Archives, North Carolina State Archives, Texas State Library and Archives Commission, Alabama Department of Archives and History, Mississippi Department of Archives and History

Search for both the veteran's pension (filed by the soldier) and the widow's pension (filed after the soldier's death) — the widow's file often contains the most family information because she had to prove the marriage and identify all children
common_mistakes: Using only the pension index card and not ordering the full file — the index contains a tiny fraction of the information in the complete file
Searching for Confederate pensions at NARA — Confederate pensions are state records, not federal. NARA holds Union pensions only
Not checking for both the veteran's and widow's files — they are separate files with different information, and both may exist for the same soldier

patterns.scenario: An ancestor served in the War of 1812, Mexican-American War, or earlier conflicts and may have received a land warrant
patterns.era_scope: 1775-1855, covering Revolutionary War through Mexican-American War service
patterns.interpretation: The federal government rewarded military service with land warrants — certificates entitling the holder to claim a specified number of acres on the public domain. These were issued for Revolutionary War, War of 1812, and Mexican-American War service, and under general bounty land acts of 1847, 1850, and 1855. Many veterans sold their warrants rather than moving west to claim land. The bounty land application file contains military service proof, age, residence, and often family details.
action_steps: Search the bounty land warrant application files at NARA (Record Group 15, Records of the Veterans Administration) — use NATF Form 85 to request the file. The application file includes the warrant itself plus supporting evidence of service

Check the Bureau of Land Management General Land Office Records (glorecords.blm.gov) for the patent issued when the warrant was used — this shows where the land was located. If the veteran sold the warrant, the patent will be in the buyer's name

Search the published indexes: 'Index of Revolutionary War Pension Applications' (NARA M805), 'War of 1812 Pension Application Files Index' (NARA M313), and 'Index to Mexican War Pension Files' (NARA T317) — all available on Ancestry and FamilySearch

For Revolutionary War veterans, also search state-level bounty land records — Virginia, North Carolina, Georgia, and other states issued their own warrants for lands in what became Kentucky, Tennessee, Ohio, and Georgia. Virginia military bounty land records are at the Library of Virginia
Cross-reference the bounty land warrant with the veteran's pension file (if any) — the two files may contain complementary information. Some veterans received both a pension and a land warrant
common_mistakes: Assuming the bounty land patent means the veteran lived on that land — many warrants were sold to speculators. The veteran might have remained in his home state while someone else settled the land
Not searching state bounty land records in addition to federal — Virginia's military district in Ohio, for example, is documented in Virginia state records, not NARA
Overlooking the 1855 Bounty Land Act, which was the most generous and extended warrants to the widows and minor children of veterans — the widow's application contains family information
source_types_to_check: bounty land warrant application files (NARA RG 15)
published pension and bounty land indexes

patterns.scenario: You know an ancestor served in the military but cannot locate their burial or detailed service information
patterns.era_scope: 1879-present, when the federal government began providing headstones for veterans
patterns.interpretation: Since 1879, the federal government has provided headstones and markers for veterans' graves. The application card for these headstones — filed by the next of kin or the cemetery — records the veteran's name, rank, unit, dates of service, date of death, and cemetery location. These cards are a valuable finding aid for locating both burial sites and service details, especially for veterans whose service records were lost in the 1973 NPRC fire.
action_steps: Search the NARA microfilm publication M1845, 'Card Records of Headstones Provided for Deceased Union Civil War Veterans, ca. 1879-ca. 1903' on Ancestry and Fold3

For post-Civil War through WWII, search the Nationwide Gravesite Locator maintained by the VA (gravelocator.cem.va.gov) — this database includes veterans buried in national cemeteries, state veterans cemeteries, and private cemeteries where the VA furnished a headstone

If you find a headstone application, use the unit information to request the Compiled Military Service Record (CMSR) from NARA — the CMSR is a jacket of card abstracts recording the soldier's service events (muster rolls, hospital records, prisoner of war records, etc.)
Visit FindAGrave (findagrave.com) and BillionGraves (billiongraves.com) for headstone photographs — military headstones often include rank, unit, and war, which can launch a service record search
For state-funded veteran headstones and markers, check the state veterans affairs office — some states maintained independent headstone programs with their own records
common_mistakes: Not using the headstone application as a pointer to the service record — the unit information on the headstone card is the key to finding the full service file
Confusing the VA's Nationwide Gravesite Locator with FindAGrave — they are separate databases with different coverage. Search both
Not checking whether the headstone was replaced — replacement applications may contain updated or corrected service information

patterns.scenario: Federal military records are incomplete or destroyed (especially post-1973 NPRC fire) and you need alternative service documentation
patterns.era_scope: 1861-1945, most useful for Civil War and state militia/National Guard records
patterns.interpretation: State adjutant general offices maintained their own military records independently of the federal government. These include muster rolls, service cards, bonus applications, discharge records, and correspondence. When NARA records are unavailable (especially Army records destroyed in the 1973 fire), state adjutant general records may be the only surviving documentation of service. Most states transferred historical records to the state archives.
action_steps: Contact the state archives in the state where the ancestor enlisted — each state's adjutant general created service records for state militia, National Guard, and state volunteers. For Civil War, this is the state the regiment was raised in

Search for published state rosters — many states published official rosters of their Civil War, Spanish-American War, and WWI soldiers. These are available at state libraries, on Google Books, and on Internet Archive. They typically list name, rank, dates of service, and sometimes casualty information

Check for state bonus records — after WWI and WWII, many states paid a bonus to returning veterans. Bonus applications (held at state archives) include service details and post-war address. Not all states had bonus programs, but those that did created valuable records

For National Guard records, contact both the state adjutant general's current office and the state archives — active-era records may still be at the adjutant general's office, while historical records are typically at the state archives

Search for regimental histories and unit rosters at the state historical society — volunteer organizations and veterans' groups often published detailed unit histories with biographical sketches of members
common_mistakes: Going straight to NARA without checking state records first — state records may be more accessible, more detailed for the specific veteran, and not affected by the 1973 fire
Not knowing which state the ancestor enlisted in — this may differ from their state of residence, especially for Civil War and WWI
Overlooking state bonus records, which are underused but provide post-war address and service confirmation
source_types_to_check: regimental histories at state historical societies

patterns.scenario: A 20th-century veteran ancestor used VA benefits (home loan, education, disability, burial) and you need location or biographical information
patterns.interpretation: The Department of Veterans Affairs (and its predecessor, the Veterans Administration) created extensive records for veterans who used benefits: VA home loan guaranty records, GI Bill education records, disability compensation claims, hospitalization records, and burial records. These records contain address information, family details, and medical history. Access is restricted for privacy — records of living veterans require the veteran's authorization; records of deceased veterans may be available to next of kin.
action_steps: For deceased veterans, request records from the VA using SF-180 through the National Personnel Records Center (nprc.nara.gov) — next of kin can obtain service and medical records. Include a copy of the death certificate
Search VA burial records through the Nationwide Gravesite Locator (gravelocator.cem.va.gov) — this covers national cemeteries and some state and private cemeteries

For VA home loan records (GI Bill home purchases), check county deed records for the property — VA-guaranteed loans are noted on the mortgage documents at the county recorder's office, confirming both veteran status and post-war address

Check state veterans homes (residential care facilities for veterans) — admission records include military service details and family contacts. These are held by the individual state facilities or state archives

Search the Veterans History Project at the Library of Congress (loc.gov/vets) for oral histories — if the veteran or a family member participated, there may be a recorded interview with extensive biographical and service information
common_mistakes: Not knowing that next of kin can access records of deceased veterans — privacy restrictions are often misunderstood as absolute bars when they are actually kinship-based access controls
Overlooking county deed records as evidence of VA benefits usage — the VA loan guaranty notation is in the county recorder's files, not VA files
Not checking the Veterans History Project, which is free and publicly accessible

source_references: National Archives and Records Administration. 'Research in Military Records at NARA.' archives.gov/research/military.
Neagles, James C. 'U.S. Military Records: A Guide to Federal and State Sources, Colonial America to the Present.' Ancestry Publishing, 1994.
NARA. 'Guide to Federal Records: Records of the Selective Service System (Record Group 163).' archives.gov/research/guide-fed-records.
National Personnel Records Center. 'Request Military Service Records.' nprc.nara.gov.
Hewett, Janet B., ed. 'The Roster of Union Soldiers, 1861-1865.' Broadfoot Publishing, 1997-2001.
